title: Man-Chain Rule and Wages — Advanced Module
objectives: Model work using men, days, and hours variables
Solve wage sharing based on work contribution
Apply proportional reasoning to inverse and direct relationships
Use man-chain formulas to simplify real aptitude problems
theory: Man-Chain Rule relates work to the number of men, number of days, and hours worked per day.
If work is constant, more men or longer hours reduce the required days; if men or hours increase, work increases proportionally.
Wage distribution is usually based on the amount of work done, not merely time spent.
Man-chain problems are an extension of Time and Work with wage allocation and multi-factor scaling.

formulas.explanation: Total output is proportional to the manpower and time invested.
With more workers, less time is needed for the same task.

formulas.formula: Wage share = individual work / total work × total payment
formulas.explanation: Payment is divided in the same ratio as the work contribution.

formulas.formula: Work share per person = rate × hours worked
formulas.explanation: A worker's output depends on both efficiency and time invested.

workedExamples.title: Man-chain direct proportionality
workedExamples.problem: 8 men finish a job in 15 days working 7 hours each day. If 12 men work 8 hours each day, how many days will they take?

workedExamples.title: Wage sharing by work done
workedExamples.problem: A and B are paid Rs. 500 for a job. A works 10 days at 8 hours/day, B works 12 days at 6 hours/day. How much does A receive?

workedExamples.title: Inverse proportionality with constant work
workedExamples.problem: If 10 men can finish a job in 12 days, how many days will 15 men take?
steps: Time is inversely proportional to men when work is constant.

questions.question: Work is directly proportional to:
questions.explanation: More men, more days, or more hours increase total work proportionally.

questions.question: If men increase, time will:
questions.explanation: More workers reduce the time needed for the same amount of work.

questions.question: Which formula is used in Man-Chain Rule?
questions.explanation: Man-chain multiplies men, days, and hours to estimate work quantity.

questions.question: Wages are distributed based on:
questions.explanation: Payment is usually shared in proportion to work contribution, not time alone.

questions.question: If 10 men complete work in 12 days, 15 men will take:
questions.explanation: Time = 12 × 10 / 15 = 8 days when work and hours are constant.

questions.question: Man-Chain Rule is based on:
questions.explanation: The rule uses proportional reasoning to relate men, days, and hours.

questions.question: If hours increase, work:
questions.explanation: More hours increase total man-hours and therefore work done.

questions.question: If men are doubled, time becomes:
questions.explanation: Doubling manpower halves the time for the same task, assuming constant output.

questions.question: Man-Chain Rule is an extension of:
questions.explanation: It extends time-and-work by adding manpower and hours factors.

questions.question: First step in solving Man-Chain problems is:
questions.explanation: Identify how each factor changes before computing actual numbers.

questions.question: A job takes 12 men 8 days at 7 hours/day. How many days will 16 men take at 6 hours/day?
questions.explanation: Total work = 12×8×7=672. New daily output = 16×6=96. Days=672/96=7.

questions.question: A and B share Rs. 600. A works 10 days at 9 hours/day, B works 12 days at 6 hours/day. What does B receive?
questions.explanation: A=90 hrs, B=72 hrs, total=162. B's share=72/162×600=270.

questions.question: A worker does 1/5 of a job in 4 days working 6 hours/day. What is his hourly work rate?
questions.explanation: Total hours=24, work=1/5 ⇒ rate=1/5 ÷ 24=1/120.

questions.question: 10 men working 8 hours a day finish a job in 9 days. How many men working 6 hours/day will finish it in 12 days?
questions.explanation: Work = 10 × 8 × 9 = 720. Required men = 720 / (6 × 12) = 10 men.

questions.question: If 8 men can finish a job in 12 days working 6 hours/day, how many days will 12 men take working 8 hours/day?
questions.explanation: Work = 8×12×6 = 576. New daily output = 12×8 = 96. Days = 576/96 = 6.

questions.question: A and B share Rs. 760. A works 10 days at 8 hours/day and B works 12 days at 6 hours/day. What is B's share?
questions.explanation: A does 80 hrs, B does 72 hrs, total 152 hrs. B's share = 72/152 × 760 = Rs. 360.

questions.question: 16 men can complete a job in 9 days working 7 hours/day. How many days will 12 men take working 8 hours/day?
A job takes 10 men 15 days working 8 hours/day. How many days will 15 men take working 10 hours/day?
A is 3 times as efficient as B. If they can finish a job together in 6 days, how many days will B alone take?
questions.explanation: Let B rate = x, A = 3x, combined = 4x. Job takes 6 days => 4x×6 = 1, so x = 1/24. B alone = 24 days.

questions.question: 12 men working 9 hours/day finish a task in 8 days. How many hours/day must 8 men work to finish it in 12 days?
questions.explanation: Work = 12×9×8 = 864. Required daily output = 864/(8×12)=9 hours per day.

questions.question: A, B, and C share wages in proportion to their work. If A does 20 man-hours, B does 15 man-hours and C does 10 man-hours, what fraction of the total wages does B receive?
A worker finishes 1/4 of a job in 5 days working 6 hours/day. To finish the whole job in 10 days, how many hours/day must he work?
questions.explanation: He does 1/4 job in 30 hrs, so full job takes 120 hrs. In 10 days he must work 120/10 = 12 hrs/day.

questions.question: If 12 men working 8 hours/day can finish a job in 5 days, how many days will 15 men working 8 hours/day take?
questions.explanation: Work = 12×8×5=480. New daily output = 15×8=120. Days = 480/120 = 4.

questions.question: A and B share wages in ratio 7:9. If A works 5 days at 6 hours/day and B works 5 days at 8 hours/day, what is the combined efficiency ratio of A:B?
questions.explanation: Rates are proportional to hours worked if days are same. A:B = 6:8 = 3:4.

questions.question: A and B are paid Rs. 248. A works 5 days at 6 hrs/day and B works 4 days at 8 hrs/day. What is B's share?
questions.explanation: A does 30 hrs, B does 32 hrs, total 62 hrs. B's share = 32/62 × 248 = Rs. 128.

questions.question: 12 men can finish a work in 9 days working 8 hours/day. How many men are needed to finish it in 6 days working 12 hours/day?
questions.explanation: Work = 12×9×8=864. Required daily output = 12×h=144. Days 6 => men = 864/(12×6) = 12.

questions.question: A worker completes 2/5 of a job in 4 days working 6 hours/day. If he works 8 hours/day, how many days will he take to complete the whole job?
questions.explanation: Current output = 24 hrs for 2/5 job, so full job = 60 hrs. At 8 hrs/day: 60/8 = 7.5 days.

questions.question: A and B working 6 hours/day can finish a job in 10 days and 15 days respectively. If they work together 8 hours/day, how many days will they take?
questions.explanation: A rate = 1/(10×6)=1/60 per hr, B = 1/90 per hr, combined = 1/36 per hr. At 8 hrs/day => 8/36 = 2/9 job/day, so 9/2=4.5 days.

questions.question: A receives 1/3 of the total wages and B receives 1/2. If A works 10 days at 8 hours/day and B works 6 hours/day, how many days does B work?
questions.explanation: A:B = 1/3 : 1/2 = 2:3. A hours = 80, so B hours = 120. At 6 hrs/day, B needs 20 days.

questions.question: A job is paid Rs. 248. A works 5 days at 6 hrs/day and B works 4 days at 8 hrs/day. How much does A receive?
questions.explanation: A does 30 hrs, B does 32 hrs, total 62 hrs. A's share = 30/62 × 248 = Rs. 120.

practiceProblems.question: 12 men can finish a job in 16 days working 7 hours a day. How many days will 9 men take working 8 hours a day?
practiceProblems.explanation: Work = 12 × 16 × 7 = 1344 man-hours. Rate = 9 × 8 = 72 man-hours/day. Days = 1344 / 72 = 18.67 days.

practiceProblems.question: A and B divide wages in proportion to work. A works 20 days at 6 hours/day, B works 15 days at 8 hours/day. They earn Rs. 1040. What is A's share?
practiceProblems.explanation: A=120 hrs, B=120 hrs, equal work share, so A gets half of 1040 = Rs. 520.

references: BRM wages and man-chain rule standard
